coredata.dc:title: Role of reactive oxygen species in the visible light photocatalytic mineralization of rhodamine B dye by P25–carbon dot photocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Mukthar Ali, Mohamed
Arya Nair, J.S.
Sandhya, K.Y.
coredata.dc:description: An undoped carbon dot (C-dot) with very high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) (25%) and the P25/C-dot composite photocatalyst with improved visible light (VL) photocatalytic activity is reported. The composite exhibits superior VL photodegradation (PD) towards the dyes (RhB) and methyl orange compared to that of control P25. The effect of reactive oxygen species (ROS) is studied in the VL PD of the dye RhB using scavengers. Our study suggests that the ROS responsible for the mineralization of RhB is hydroxyl radical (•OH) and the combination of •OH and singlet oxygen (1O2) has a synergistic effect on the mineralization of RhB. The •OH formation is thought to be through the secondary reactions of ROS. Enhancement in the VL PD and the formation of •OH are attributed to the presence of C-dot. The results suggest that C-dot assists the formation of •OH through secondary reactions.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PD studies of RhB, MO and 4CP
PD studies in the presence of scavengers
Determination of •OH by fluorescent probe method
VL PD studies of P25/C-dot using RhB, MO and 4CP
Formation of ROS in the PD of RhB

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A & B): HRTEM images (C): SAED pattern and (D): size distribution of C-dot.
(A): UV–Vis absorption spectra and (B): PL with excitation from 340 to 450 nm (inset: photoimage of C-dot under UV irradiation).
XPS spectra corresponding to wide, C1s, O1s and Ti2p of P25/C-dot.

(A): The HRTEM images (red circles indicate the C-dots) of P25/C-dot; (B): The FTIR spectra with inset showing the peaks corresponding to C-dot), (C): TGA done in the air atmosphere and (D): the UV–Vis absorption spectra of P25 and P25/C-dot. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
VL PD of (A) RhB and (B) MO by (a): no catalyst; (b): P25 and (c): P25/C-dot.
(A) Scavenger studies for P25/C-dot in the PD of RhB with (a): no scavenger; (b): t-BuOH; (c): NaN3 and (d): BQ. (B). The effect of scavengers in the PD of RhB.
PL spectra of HTA in the presence of (A): P25/C-dot; (B): P25 and (C): PL intensity of HTA with respect to time in the presence of TA.
Scheme for the VL PD of RhB via the de-ethylated mechanism.
UV–Visible spectra of RhB during PD with (A) no scavenger; (B) t-BuOH; (C): NaN3 and (D): BQ along with P25/C-dot under VL irradiation.
Scavenger studies with (a): no scavenger; (b): t-BuOH and (c): NaN3. (B). Bar graph indicating the scavenger effect in the presence of PD P25/C-dot in the PD of MO.
Recycling efficiency of P25/C-dot for the PD of RhB.
Preparation of P25/C-dot composite from P25 and C-dot mixing.
Mechanism for the formation of ROS by P25/C-dot under VL PD.
Percent contribution of ROS in the VL PD of RhB by P25/C-dot and P25.

head.ce:title: Role of reactive oxygen species in the visible light photocatalytic mineralization of rhodamine B dye by P25–carbon dot photocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Space Science and Technology, Valiyamala, Thiruvananthapuram, 695547, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Institute of Space Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An undoped carbon dot (C-dot) with very high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) (25%) and the P25/C-dot composite photocatalyst with improved visible light (VL) photocatalytic activity is reported. The composite exhibits superior VL photodegradation (PD) towards the dyes (RhB) and methyl orange compared to that of control P25. The effect of reactive oxygen species (ROS) is studied in the VL PD of the dye RhB using scavengers. Our study suggests that the ROS responsible for the mineralization of RhB is hydroxyl radical (•OH) and the combination of •OH and singlet oxygen (1O2) has a synergistic effect on the mineralization of RhB. The •OH formation is thought to be through the secondary reactions of ROS. Enhancement in the VL PD and the formation of •OH are attributed to the presence of C-dot. The results suggest that C-dot assists the formation of •OH through secondary reactions.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dot (C-dot), a new class of carbon nanomaterial, which has opened up lots of interests in energy and biology-related fields due to its high aqueous solubility, chemical inertness, electronic, optical, photoluminescence (PL) and up-conversion properties [1–3]. The PL properties of C-dot depend on its size, the extent of functionalization, surface passivation and doping which in-turn can be can be controlled by the type of precursors and the synthetic methods [3,4] employed. C-dot with higher PL quantum yield (PLQY) is desirable for the applications in bio-imaging [5], photocatalytic [6], light emitting devices [7] and photovoltaics [8] etc. Many methods have been adopted to prepare C-dot, namely arc discharge [9], chemical and electrochemical oxidation [10,11], pyrolysis [12] and hydrothermal [13]. Despite the different methods, undoped C-dot exhibited lower PLQY ≤10% [14].

It was observed that the smaller the size distribution of C-dot, larger the PLQY [15]. C-dot prepared from carbohydrates precursors are expected to give higher material yield and PLQY due to the higher carbon content and hydroxyl functional groups. Undoped C-dot of ∼5 nm size prepared from glucose [16] using the ultrasonic method showed a PLQY of 7%. Carbonization of sucrose [17] using sulfuric acid lead to the formation of two differently sized C-dot; one with the size of ∼5 nm and blue emission and the other with size ranging from 1 to 2 nm and green emission. Similarly hydrothermal treatment of different β-cyclodextrins (CD) in hydrochloric acid at 70 °C yielded C-dots with 1.7–3.3 nm in size and the PLQY of 9.7%, 13.5% and 10.6% for the C-dots obtained from α, β and γ-cyclodextrins respectively [18]. It has been reported that for the same precursor, different preparation methods yielded differently sized C-dot and thus different PLQY [19,20]. For instance, microwave-assisted synthesis of C-dot (19) from citric acid yielded 11% PLQY while hydrothermal based [20] synthesis yielded 25.7% PLQY. Recently Fang et al. [21] reported a simple synthesis for the preparation of C-dot from acetic acid using P2O5-water method which resulted in 2.3% of PLQY. By combining this method with a carbon-rich precursor, (which is CD), we are able to prepare undoped C-dot with higher PLQY (∼25%). There are very few reports on the preparation of undoped C-dot with very high PLQY, i.e. around 43% [22].

The photodegradation (PD) of dyes is one of the viable option to eliminate the carbon foot-print from the environment. The PD mechanism is largely dependent on the type of reactive oxygen species (ROS) involved in the process [23] which in turn related to the photocatalyst, the source of light irradiation and also the type of dyes involved. In this paper, we report a new and simple preparation of undoped C-dot with high PLQY from CD. The C-dot exhibits ∼25% PLQY which is one of the highest value reported for undoped C-dot. The P25/C-dot composite prepared by physisorption method displays improved visible light (VL) photocatalytic properties against methyl orange (MO) and rhodamine B (RhB) dyes. The role of ROS in the PD of the RhB is carried out under VL PD conditions. The extended VL absorption of C-dot in the composite is credited for the enhanced PD properties and the significantly higher amount of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2) formation are assigned to the mineralization. The study suggests that •OH is crucial and the combination of •OH and 1O2 have a synergistic effect in the mineralization of RhB.

P25 (aeroxide), benzoquinone (BQ), 4-chlorophenol (4CP), RhB and β-cyclodextrin (CD) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich India Co. Ltd. Phosphorous pentoxide (P2O5), methyl orange (MO), terephthalic acid (TA), tertiary butyl alcohol (t-BuOH) and sodium azide (NaN3) were purchased from Merck, India Ltd. Distilled water was used for all the experiments.

To the CD (150 mg), solid P2O5 (3 g) powder was added and mixed well by stirring. Distilled water (1 ml) was added slowly to the mixture till the effervescence was subdued. The colour changed from white to black. This was due to the carbonization of CD by the high exothermic heat generated during the reaction between P2O5 and water. The mixture was diluted to 10 ml to make the stock solution.

To 10 ml of C-dot stock solution, 300 mg of P25 was added in 25 ml beaker and sonicated for 15 min (min). After that the suspension was stirred overnight at room temperature. Finally, the precipitate was washed and centrifuged plenty of times with water. Then the precipitate was dried at 70 °C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PD studies of RhB, MO and 4CP
ce:section.ce:para: Stock solutions of RhB (0.015 mg/ml), MO (0.02 mg/ml) and 4CP (0.01 mg/ml) were used for the PD studies. Typically, 17 mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed in 25 ml of the RhB stock solution. The mixture was bath sonicated for 2 min and then the suspension was stirred for 60 min under dark conditions for attaining adsorption equilibrium. Under ambient conditions, the suspensions were irradiated with VL (>400 nm) using 84 W light sources. For determining PD, photocatalyst was removed by centrifugation prior to the recording of UV–Vis spectra and the PD of RhB, MO and 4CP were monitored from the absorbance values at 554, 460 and 224 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PD studies in the presence of scavengers
ce:section.ce:para: An aqueous solution (50 mM) of t-BuOH, NaN3 and BQ were prepared and used as stock solutions for the scavenging studies. To the 17 mg of the photocatalyst, 25 ml of the stock solution of RhB was added followed by the addition of 2.7 ml of the corresponding scavenger stock solution. Rest of the procedure was similar to that of described in PD studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of •OH by fluorescent probe method
ce:section.ce:para: A stock solution of TA (1.71 mM) was prepared using a 4 mM aqueous NaOH solution. From this stock solution, 25 ml was added to 17 mg of the P25/C-dot composite. The suspension was sonicated for 2 min and then stirred for 60 min under dark condition. Then the mixture was irradiated with VL (84 W) and the reaction was monitored using spectrofluorimeter. The excitation and emission wavelengths were 310 and 425 nm, respectively. The actual PD% in the absence of scavenger was normalized to 100% and the PD% in the presence of scavengers was recalculated accordingly from which the percentage contribution of each reactive oxygen species (ROS) towards the PD was estimated.

To check the photo-stability of the prepared photocatalyst, recycling studies were carried out using the solar simulator as the light source. Typically, 4.5 mg of the catalyst was dispersed in 20 ml of RhB solution (10 μM) followed by 10 min of bath sonication. The suspension was stirred under dark condition for 60 min to attain the adsorption equilibrium. Then the mixture was irradiated with the solar simulator lamp (150W of Xenon lamp) with mild stirring for 60 min which was taken as one cycle. The PD % was calculated from the absorbance spectra of RhB (λ max = 554 nm) at 0 and 60 min, respectively. The same procedure was repeated for six consecutive cycles.

The Fourier transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded in a Perkin Elmer spectrum 100 FTIR spectrophotometer. Solid-state PL spectra were recorded in Horiba FluoroMax-4 with the excitation and emission slits of 1 and 5 nm respectively. For 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (HTA) detection, excitation and emission slits of 1 and 2 nm, respectively, were used. Absorption spectra of the solutions were recorded using CARY100 Bio UV–visible spectrophotometer. Solid-state diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded out in a Shimadzu 2600 instrument. Raman spectroscopic measurements were carried out in a Renishaw inVia Raman microscope with the excitation laser wavelength of 532 nm. Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) were done using a TA Q50 instrument. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) analyses were done using JOEL JEM2100 electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were carried out using Kratos analytical axis ultra-X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with the excitation source of Al-Kα. Photoelectrochemical characterizations were carried out using Autolab PGstat320N from Metrohm. Gas chromatography – mass spectrometer (GCMS) studies were carried out in The JEOL GCMATE II GC-MS with Data system is a high resolution, double focusing instrument with a maximum resolution of 6000 Da.

An illustration of the preparation of C-dot and P25/C-dot composite are given in Scheme 1 . The C-dot is prepared by mixing the aqueous dispersion of CD with P2O5. The instant heat energy produced during the mixing of P2O5 with water in the presence of CD is expected to be the cause of the formation of C-dot (21). The P25/C-dot composite was made by mixing the aqueous dispersions of the C-dot and P25. The HRTEM images of the C-dot (Fig. 1 A and B) revealed a uniform C-dot (18) with an average size of 3.7 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. 1D). The diffusive rings in the SAED pattern (Fig. 1C) suggest amorphous nature for the C-dot. The lattice fringes of graphene layers in C-dot are not visible since it is amorphous in nature.

Optical characterization is an important tool to investigate the electronic excitations of the materials. Studies on the optical properties of C-dot are done using UV–Visible and fluorescence spectroscopy and the results are given in Fig. 2 . The two peaks in the absorption spectra of C-dot at 280 and 365 nm (Fig. 2A), can be arising from the π-π* transition and the oxygen related surface states corresponding to n-π* transition of C=O bond, respectively [24,25]. The strong blue PL emission (inset of Fig. 2B) of C-dot demonstrates its higher PLQY which suggests C-dot's higher aqueous solubility. The C-dot has limited aggregation in water and is attributed to the presence of carboxylic acid and hydroxyl functional groups. The emission of C-dot remains unchanged (∼435 nm) for the excitation wavelengths from 340 to 420 nm. Though the excitation dependent PL of C-dot has been widely reported, only a few reports of excitation independent PL by C-dot are available [26,27]. The excitation dependent PL arises due to the heterogeneity in the size and the surface states of the C-dots [28], whereas the excitation independent PL [17] is an indication of uniform morphology and surface states of C-dot. This property of C-dot agrees with the uniform size of C-dot revealed by The TEM images. The PL lifetime of C-dot is determined using time-correlated single photon count at the excitation wavelength of 344 nm (Fig. S1). The PL lifetime of C-dot is ∼2.77 ns with a bi-exponential decay fitting and the PLQY is calculated to be 25%. The details of the calculation are given in the supplementary file. The obtained PLQY value is one of the highest value for the undoped C-dot so far reported. Previous reports showed that the C-dot prepared from CD (18) using hydrothermal method showed a PLQY of 13.5% while the C-dot made by P2O5-water method using acetic acid as precursor recounted a lower PLQY of 2.3% (21). This shows that the combination of P2O5-water method and CD precursor has improved the PLQY value (25%) of C-dot, considerably, and is partially attributed to the uniform size, and hence seems to be a better approach to achieve C-dot with higher PLQY.

Further the P25/C-dot photocatalyst prepared is characterized. To understand the structural interaction between C-dot and P25 in the composite, XPS analyses are carried out and the spectra are given in Fig. 3 . The wide spectrum of P25/C-dot (Fig. 3) shows the peaks for C, Ti and O elements. In the deconvolution spectrum of C1s, three different carbon environments are observed from the peaks at 284.8, 286 and 288.9 eV and are assigned to the C1s of C=C-, CO and C=O bonds, respectively. No TiC bond peak is observed which indicates the absence of C-doping. The peaks at 529.6, 530.1 and 531.7 eV correspond to the O1s deconvolution and are ascribed to TiOC, C=O and COH, respectively. Ti2p deconvoluted peaks at 458.7 and 464.4 eV correspond to the Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2 of P25, respectively. Thus, the XPS results confirm the formation of P25/C-dot and suggest that interaction between C-dot and P25 exist through TiOC bonding, could be through dissociative bonding.

The HRTEM analysis shows the morphologies of P25 and C-dot (Fig. 4 A). The C-dot adsorbed on the edges of P25 are shown in the images (as red circles), indicate the presence of C-dot which is further confirmed by the EDS spectrum (Figs. S2 and S3). The EDS mapping indicates that the distribution of C-dot in the photocatalyst is more or less uniform. The lattice fringe distance of 0.358 nm corresponds to (101) planes of P25 are clearly seen in the HRTEM images (Fig. S4). The FTIR spectra of CD, C-dot, P25 and P25/C-dot are given in Fig. 4B. The peaks at 3350 and 1645 cm−1 in both the P25 and P25/C-dot are attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of surface hydroxyl (OH) groups, respectively. Further the inset of P25/C-dot (Fig. 4B) clearly shows the presence of COC stretching (1381 cm−1) of C-dot [18,29]. The peak at ∼805 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of P25/C-dot is assigned to the TiOC stretching and thus supports the formation of surface bonding between TiO2 and C-dot [30] (Fig. S5). The TiOC formation between TiO2 and C-dot may possibly due to the COOH or the OH groups of C-dot. The amount of C-dot loading is determined using TGA (Fig. 4C) in the air atmosphere. The weight loss from room temperature to 200 °C is assigned to the removal of water molecules and that of from 200 to 550 °C to the removal of oxygen functional groups. The difference in the residual amount, ∼2.2%, gives a rough estimate of the weight% of C-dot, which is ≥ 2.2%. The absorption spectra of P25 and P25/C-dot are given in Fig. 4D and it is interesting to note that the absorption of P25 is up to ∼400 nm, while that of P25/C-dot is up to ∼700 nm. This is a clear evidence for the presence of C-dot and the extension of absorption to the visible region (500–700 nm) by the composite is greater than that of the bare C-dot (Fig. 2) and is a clear indication of the interaction between P25 and C-dot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: VL PD studies of P25/C-dot using RhB, MO and 4CP
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the performance of P25/C-dot photocatalyst towards a colorless compound, VL PD of 4CP was performed and the results are given in Fig. S7. The performance of P25/C-dot (49%) is marginally better than P25 (46%) in 4CP VL PD. The comparable VL PD of 4CP by P25 is mainly due to the ligand to metal charge transfer process between P25 and the phenolic group of 4CP [31,32].

The advantageous of P25/C-dot over the other photocatalysts is due to the tunable electronic structure of C-dot by changing the size and the surface passivation which allows the P25/C-dot heterojunction with suitable band-energy alignment. Further, conjugated π structure of C-dot facilitating the surface active sites on P25 and the surface functional groups strongly bound on P25 surface, favor the charge transfer with better photostability [25,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of ROS in the PD of RhB
ce:section.ce:para: The notable difference between P25 and P25/C-dot is the significant formation of •OH (34%) in the presence of the latter. Though previously we have reported the VL PD of different composites such as TiO2 (001)-graphene [31], NTiO2 (34) and the NTiO2 MoS2 (35) with extended absorption in the VL region, except the TiO2 (001)-graphene composite, none of them produced •OH in their PD reactions of RhB, however all produced the O2•−. The formation of •OH by TiO2 (001)-graphene is assigned to the excitation of electrons from the valence band (VB) of TiO2 to the intra-state formed just below the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 (due to C-doping). This means that the photogenerated holes are produced in the VB of TiO2. The other two photocatalysts mentioned did not produce •OH under the VL PD conditions [36], as the excitation of TiO2 was not possible.

Identification of intermediates formed during the VL PD of RhB using P25/C-dot (Fig. S9) is studied using the GCMS. GCMS graphs are plotted with detection intensity in y-axis against m/z or retention time (RT) in the x-axis. Peaks are noted with their retention time (RT) in minute (min) and the PD of RhB by P25/C-dot yielded the multiple intermediates. Peaks at the RTs 12.65, 14.73 and 15.92 min confirm as benzoic acid; 2, 3-dihydroxy benzaldehyde and 1, 3-benzene dicarboxylic acid respectively, based on their molecular ion peaks and mass fragmentation pattern. However, we could not assign the chemical structure of other intermediates based on the fragmentation pattern. The formation of primitive benzene based derivatives are strongly supported by previous literature [37,38]. The primitive benzene based intermediates are suggestive of de-ethylation mechanism followed by mineralization pathway.

Further, the formation of the higher amount of •OH by P25/C-dot is confirmed by a PL probe study using TA as the probe molecule. TA is a non-fluorescent molecule which can react with •OH with high trapping efficiency (∼80%) to produce a fluorescent molecule, 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (HTA) [39,40], which is stable. The reaction scheme for the formation of HTA is given in Fig. S10. The PL spectra (Fig. 7 A and B) of HTA in the presence of P25/C-dot and P25, show that the formation of HTA is slower and lesser with P25 as compared to that of P25/C-dot. The PL intensity is nil at 0 min for both and on VL irradiation, the formation of HTA increased ∼370 times for P25/C-dot, whereas the increase is ∼143 times for P25, which is an evidence of the higher amount of •OH formation by P25/C-dot.

The scavenger studies reveal that the high redox potential species such as •OH, O2•−, and 1O2 are formed by P25/C-dot, while low redox potential O2•− is only formed by P25 (Table 1) during the VL PD of RhB. The ROS formed is a major factor that influences the PD pathway of the dye. Therefore, in an attempt to understand the role of the ROS in the PD pathway, the absorption spectra of RhB during PD by P25/C-dot in the absence and presence of scavengers are examined (Fig. 9). The major difference here is the shift in the λ max of the PD mixture which is an indication of the difference in the PD mechanism. Typically, the PD of RhB is initiated by a step-wise removal of the four ethyl groups from the molecule i.e. de-ethylation process (Fig. 8
.) followed by the cleavage of chromophores [41–43].

Thus there are two steps [44] in the PD of RhB namely decolouration and mineralization pathway which depend on the redox potential of the ROS involved. If the oxidizing power of the ROS is not high enough to cleave the chromophore, they may initiate the de-ethylation of RhB and end up in the accumulation of de-ethylated product (hereafter referred to as the ‘decolouration pathway’). If ROS with higher redox potential are involved, they proceed to the second step, depending on the kinetics (referred to as mineralization pathway) after the de-ethylation step, which thus can lead to mineralization of RhB and hence negligible or no accumulation of de-ethylated product happens, leading to no shift in λ max. . In the absence of scavenger, no shift in the λ max (at 554 nm) is observed (Fig. 9A), which indicates that mineralization happens i.e., the kinetics of mineralization step is faster. However, in the presence of t-BuOH, where •OH is scavenged, (Fig. 9B), a hypsochromic shift (5 nm) is observed which indicates that decolouration happens and a pronounced hypsochromic shift (∼15 nm) (Fig. 9C) in the presence of NaN3 (where both 1O2 and •OH are scavenged) further ascertains the role of •OH in the mineralization. In the presence of BQ, no shift (Fig. 9D) is observed where O2•− is scavenged. In other words, non-scavenged •OH and 1O2 cause the mineralization over decolouration. This is the reason for no shift in the presence of BQ. Though O2•− can cause significant photodegradation, it causes mainly through the decolouration pathway of RhB (de-ethylated RhB) because of its comparatively lower reduction potential. Whereas •OH and 1O2 can cause mineralization over decolouration. This is the reason in the presence of BQ, there is a significant photodegradation efficiency but lesser towards mineralization pathway. While a ∼5 nm shift is observed when •OH alone scavenged it is ∼15 nm in the absence of both 1O2 and •OH, which suggests that in the presence of both there is a synergistic effect for mineralization.

Further, the formation of • OH (and 1O2) by P25/C-dot, is confirmed by scavenging studies in the VL PD reaction of MO (Fig. 10 ). The contribution of • OH and 1O2 are 74 and 13%, respectively. The result confirms the formation of • OH by P25/C-dot during the VL PD reactions.

It is clear from the results that P25/C-dot produces •OH while the production of •OH by P25 is negligible and the previous reports too confirm that •OH formation during VL PD reactions is not common [32]. The holes with an energy level of >2.33 eV vs NHE can only oxidize the water directly to form •OH and this is possible only if TiO2 is excited which requires UV light (<380 nm) source, usually. Though C-dot is VL active, the holes formed in the VB (1.9 eV) of C-dot do not have sufficient potential to oxidize water to •OH. Therefore, the formation of •OH can be explained [2,3,45–49] (i) through up-conversion PL of C-dot and (ii) by secondary reactions of the primary ROS (O2•−). The up-conversion PL of C-dots is in the absorption region of TiO2 and hence it can excite the electrons from the VB of TiO2 (6, 45) and the holes formed can oxidize the water to •OH. However, the up-conversion of PL has been challenged by few reports (47, 48). Therefore, another possible way to explain •OH formation is through the secondary reactions of O2•− and was previously reported in our work [50]. The reactions are as follows [44,51],
(1) C-dot + hν (VL) → h+ (c-dot) + e− (c-dot)
(2) O2+ e− (c-dot) → O2•−
(3) O2•− + H2O →•OOH + OH−
(4) •OOH + •OOH →H2O2+ 1O2
(5) e− (c-dot) + H2O2 →•OH + OH−

Based on the results, an illustration is proposed (Scheme 2 ) to explain the formation of •OH and other ROS using the energy levels of P25 and C-dot. On excitation of P25/C-dot with VL, electrons and holes are produced from C-dot, respectively, in the CB (−0.5 eV vs NHE) and VB (1.9 eV vs NHE). The electrons in the CB (−0.5 eV vs NHE) of C-dot eventually get transferred to the CB of P25 (−0.4 eV vs NHE) or get scavenged by adsorbed O2. In addition to these other mechanisms such as dye-sensitization mechanism by colored or surface sensitization by colorless dye [52] are possible. The electrons in TiO2 too are scavenged by adsorbed O2 to form O2•− (E°O2/O2•− = −0.33 eV vs NHE). This O2•− on secondary reactions yield •OH or 1O2 (E°O2•−/1O2 = 0.65 eV vs NHE) as shown by equations (5) and (6) respectively,
(6) O2•− + h+ (P25/c-dot) →1O2

Our previous studies and this work showed that •OH is produced only in TiO2/graphene [31,32,34,35,44] composites. Other modifications did not produce •OH. This raises the question that why TiO2 C-dot or TiO2-graphene promotes the secondary reactions which produce •OH [25]. Further understanding about the role of graphene or C-dot in the generation of •OH is required. Therefore, it can be thought that graphene or C-dot is promoting the secondary reactions which lead to •OH by assisting the oxidation of H2O2 (eqn. (5)).

Recycling studies are done for RhB dye and the results are given in Fig. 11 . The recycling studies show no decrease in the photocatalytic activity for the seven cycles and are an indication of the stability of the photocatalyst.

An undoped C-dot of uniform size with high PLQY (25%) is achieved using a new approach. The P25/C-dot composite prepared using the C-dot exhibits an improved VL PD on the dyes MO and RhB and further facilitates the mineralization of RhB. The improved activity is attributed to the extended VL absorption properties of the composite. Mineralization of RhB is found to be due to •OH. Our result shows that the P25/C-dot forms •OH which plays a major role in the mineralization pathway for RhB dye. A synergistic effect is observed in the presence of •OH and 1O2 for the mineralization of RhB. The formation of •OH is attributed to the secondary reactions of O2•− and our results suggest that C-dot play a crucial role in the formation of •OH.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Final - D_spl_P - Supplementary Final - D_spl_P - Supplementary

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2018.11.057.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite
